quoted.text:
Dont confuse inventory timing with fundamentals healing.  

Were in a much worse position now than we were in February. The buffers have been drawn down hard. If another shock hits (war escalation, major disruption, or black swan) theres very little cushion left to absorb it.  

Hell, even in the base case with no new shock, the system has already taken structural damage. Higher prices or negative second-order effects later this year and into 2027 are highly probable.   

Diplomacy and temporary buffers are only stretching the timeline. They cant fully erase the deficit and damage...  

#OOTT #WTI #brent #geopolitics #hormuz
quoted.author: VonburyResearch

quoted.text:
BREAKING: JD Vance just admitted the Iran "ceasefire" is a strategic pause to refill oil markets—not a path to peace—while Washington keeps military options open.

Speaking on the Michael Knowles Show, Vance said the Trump administration is using the MOU "to sort of refill the world's oil economy… and then to see where the hand is."

He described two options still "very much in play": a long-term deal requiring a "significant change in Iranian behavior"—or "banking our wins" from the military campaign and "doing things on top of that if the president feels that we have to," referring to renewed strikes.

Vance said Trump is letting it play out "in an environment where there is significantly less pressure on the world energy economy… while not giving up a single one of his gains and while preserving a lot of optionality."

Translation: the U.S. is using the 60-day pause to restock energy supplies and military positions—while keeping the option to restart the war. The ceasefire isn't a peace agreement. It's a tactical pause. The U.S. is refilling its arsenal, not changing its strategy.
quoted.author: GulfLetter

Market analysis for July 01: Crude oil has lost its "war risk premium":

-Brent crude oil has fallen below $72/b for the first time since the end of February. 

-It has now completely erased its "Middle East war risk premium" or "Hormuz supply disruption premium". #oott #oil #war https://t.co/0rFXTFYwsE

In the first 4 months of 2026, US total oil production (crude, condensates and NGLs) has averaged 21.33m b/d — that's >900,000 b/d higher than in same period of 2025. And that's before the US shale industry had the opportunity to react to (and hedge) ~$100 a barrel.

Note seeing vessels "traffic evenly split by direction".

Thx @kpler

#oott

quoted.text:
Hormuz traffic holds steady

The Strait of Hormuz remained open and active on 30 June, with 34 verified crossings recorded and traffic evenly split by direction. The dataset showed a broad mix of commercial, energy-linked and support movements, while route visibility remained fragmented across Iranian, Omani, IMO and Dark or Unknown routes. That pattern points to continued operational continuity, but not a settled return to normal routing. With IMO’s incident tracker now listing 49 confirmed regional incidents, including a newly confirmed physical attack on BOCHEM MARENGO, maritime-security commitments linked to US-Iran discussions face a more demanding test.

Stay ahead of the market with #Kpler Insight: https://t.co/3ZkSyyYl7X
quoted.author: Kpler

@crudechronicle Oil demand is roughly income elastic. Both the GFC and Covid resulted in parallel shifts downward in these upward-sloping curves. Remains to be seen what the residual impact of this year's drama will be, but absent these events, demand would be noticeably higher.

text:
🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of pre-war levels (and some tanker trackers have UAE June oil exports even above pre-war levels).

With exports surging, the recovery in oil wellhead production won't be far behind, me thinks.

Importantly, UAE oil is flowing well beyond its typical trade partners. With Chinese demand down, Abu Dhabi is shipping lots of crude into Europe and the US.
@VKMacro The above is based on crude and condensates
